LA3 Analysis Sprint

NLU Pilot T1 v6trans · n=27 · 2026-04-21

  27. 27. Kruskal-Wallis by ARC state

  30. 30. Exemplar S11: near-median profile
  31. 31. Exemplar S14: ARC-stressed (highest relatedness frustration, T=85)

1. Methodology

  • Cohort: n=27 NLU students, baseline timepoint "NLU Pilot T1 v6trans" (2026-04). These are the students who completed the full C2A battery; 46 students were assigned to the timepoint, 27 finished.
  • Scale: all figures on T-score display scale (M=50, SD=10). Unified display + DDx bands as of 2026-04-21: Start Here T<40, Growing 40≤T<55, Strength T≥55, matching the live C2A product (packages/assessment-engine/src/config/thresholds.ts).
  • Skills: 12 durable skills across 4 canonical C2A pillars (Creative & Curious Thinkers, Leaders with Purpose & Agency, Thrivers in Change, Connectors & Collaborators).
  • DDx axes: Engine tier (0/1/2, count of constrained Engine subdomains, clamped) × ARC state (start_here / growing / strength). Engine is the 3-subdomain cognitive-control layer: working memory + inhibition (ADEXI) + cognitive flexibility (C2A-constructed). GMS circuit breaker excludes cascade-positive students from the 3×3 grid.
  • Inferential framing: descriptive, non-parametric (Kruskal-Wallis + ε²), no multiple-testing correction. Effect sizes lead; p-values reported for rigor.
  • Unfamiliar terms? See the Glossary slide. T-score, Engine tier, ARC state, cascade, silhouette, dip test, and others are all defined there. The next slide (Statistical methods at a glance) explains what each test asks and how to read its outputs.

2. Statistical methods at a glance

  • Kruskal-Wallis (KW) test. Non-parametric test of "do these groups differ on this outcome?" Used here because n=27 is too small to trust parametric ANOVA. Outputs an H statistic (higher = more separation), a p-value (below 0.05 = unlikely by chance), and an ε² effect size (0 to 1). Conventional thresholds: ε²≥0.01 small, ≥0.06 medium, ≥0.14 large (Tomczak & Tomczak, 2014). Used on the Kruskal-Wallis by Engine tier and by ARC state slides later in the deck.
  • Shapiro-Wilk normality test. Asks "is this distribution consistent with a bell curve?" Low p (<0.05) suggests non-normal. At n=27 the test has limited power and detects modest departures. In this deck, 6 of 12 per-skill distributions are p<0.05; we treat this as descriptive nuance, not a reason to abandon T-score comparisons.
  • Hartigan dip test. Asks "is this distribution unimodal (one peak) or multimodal (two or more peaks)?" Low p (<0.05) suggests multimodality. High p (what we see on all four pillar means plus PC1 here, all p>0.5) means no evidence for two populations hiding in the data.
  • K-means silhouette score. When we ask "how many clusters fit best?", silhouette scores how cleanly each student sits inside its assigned cluster (range -1 to 1; higher = tighter). We scan K=2..6 and report each score. In this cohort K=2 (silhouette 0.40) beats K=3 (0.26), so the data is best described as two groups, not three.
  • PCA (Principal Component Analysis). Compresses the 12-dimensional skill space into a 2D scatter that preserves the most variance. PC1 is the direction of maximum spread. Used for the biplot and to set up the dip test on PC1.
  • Pearson vs Spearman correlation. Pearson measures linear co-movement; Spearman measures rank co-movement (robust to outliers and non-linearities). Reporting both flags pairs where a few outliers drive the Pearson value.
  • Effect size > p-value. At n=27 we lead narration with effect sizes (ε², silhouette, mean differences) rather than p-values. A small cohort can produce a significant p on a small effect or miss a real effect with a non-significant p; the effect size captures the practical size of the difference.

3. Executive summary

  • Who is in this data. 27 of 46 NLU students completed the full C2A baseline battery in April 2026. Every one of them is in every chart. No students were dropped from analyses and no missing answers were filled in by estimate (no sampling, no imputation).
  • The headline, in one sentence. Students in this cohort score well on the 12 durable skills when measured directly, but underneath those scores two supports are soft: cognitive control (how reliably the brain's working memory, impulse control, and attention-shifting hold up under load) and psychological fuel (how much of the time students feel independent, connected, and capable). That combination (strong skills on top, soft Engine and ARC underneath) is exactly the pattern this battery is built to detect. The rest of this slide walks through each layer.
  • Durable skills: strong tops, coachable middles, small tails. The per-skill bar chart later in this deck (slide 7, now labeled Strength / Growing / Start Here) shows all three bands at once. Every one of the 12 skills has a majority of students in the top Strength band (T≥55, where 50 is the published national average and 10 points is one standard deviation), running from 16 to 22 students out of 27 on any given skill. The middle Growing band (40≤T<55) holds between 4 and 9 students per skill. These are the coachable-opportunity students. They are at or above the published national average on the skill but not yet clearing the Strength threshold, so there is real headroom for coaching to move them up. Across the 12 skills there are 77 student-skill cells in Growing; at program scale that translates to meaningful movement potential if the intervention works. Four skills have zero students in the bottom Start Here band (T<40): collaboration, critical thinking, curiosity, social awareness. The three skills with the biggest Start Here counts are networking, creative problem solving, and communication, and even those carry only 2 or 3 students each. Read as a whole: this cohort is not starting from scratch on durable skills, and there is real coachable headroom on every skill for the Growing-band students.
  • Engine layer (cognitive control) is uniformly soft. "Engine" is the C2A name for the three cognitive-control subdomains that sit under every other skill: working memory (holding information in mind), inhibition (not acting on the first impulse), and cognitive flexibility (switching between tasks or frames). Working memory and inhibition are measured with the ADEXI instrument; cognitive flexibility is a C2A-built measure. Group averages: working memory M=43.7 (SD 11.0), inhibition M=42.8 (SD 11.3), cognitive flexibility M=42.1 (SD 11.7). Plain English: the group sits about 0.7 standard deviations below the national average on all three (SDs around 11 mean this cohort's spread is roughly equal to the reference population's), which puts the typical student in this cohort in roughly the bottom third of the reference population on each. Per-subdomain Start Here counts (T<40): working memory 11, inhibition 9, cognitive flexibility 10 of 27. The Engine tier composite counts how many of the three subdomains a student is constrained on (scoring below T=55), clamped at 2: 1 at tier 0, 0 at tier 1, 26 at tier 2. Put simply, 26 of 27 students are constrained on at least one Engine subdomain, and 26 of those are constrained on two or three subdomains at once. In practice, almost the entire cohort is constrained on multiple Engine subdomains at the same time. Driver: Inhibition and cognitive flexibility are tied as the most commonly constrained subdomain (25/26 tier-1-or-2 students, 96%). Coaching implication: executive-function scaffolding will benefit nearly every student, so approaches that lift the whole layer (routines, external memory aids, structured transitions) will go further than single-subdomain targeting.
  • Fuel layer (ARC) shows the widest strain on relatedness. "ARC" is the C2A shorthand for the three psychological needs from Self-Determination Theory: autonomy (feeling you have voice and control over what you do), relatedness (feeling connected to the people around you), and competence (feeling capable and effective). Each need has two sides: how much it is being met (satisfaction) and how much it is being thwarted (frustration). Students fall into one of four combinations per need: thriving (high satisfaction, low frustration), disengaged (low on both), conflicted (high on both), or crisis (low satisfaction, high frustration). Counts per need (thriving / disengaged / conflicted / crisis): autonomy 6 / 13 / 3 / 5, relatedness 10 / 5 / 3 / 9, competence 12 / 2 / 6 / 7. Rolled into a single ARC state that requires all three needs met simultaneously to reach Strength: 2 students in Strength, 11 in Growing, 14 in Start Here. Driver: Relatedness is the single need pushing the largest share of students out of Strength (9/25 of the non-Strength students are in the crisis quadrant on this need, 36%). Coaching implication: belonging and connection are the place where this cohort most often gets stuck, so interventions that strengthen relational supports will land more often on average than ones that lean on autonomy or competence levers. This is a hypothesis coach-reported data can confirm at T3.
  • Clinical flag did not fire. The GMS cascade is a safety check built into the battery: it overrides the standard coaching routing and flags a student for clinical review when motivation is broadly absent as a dominant orientation and psychological needs are being actively thwarted at the same time (full trigger in the Glossary). No student in this cohort tripped it. Standard coaching cases: 27. Clinical stabilization cases: 0. The flag behaves as designed and did not false-positive on this cohort.
  • Two natural groups, not three. When we let the data sort students into clusters without telling it how many groups to find, a two-group split comes out cleanest (a higher-scoring core and a lower-scoring subset). Silhouette scoring favors the 2-group split over 3 or more (definition on the Statistical methods slide). Three- or four-group solutions do not hold up at this sample size. For T1 planning, expect two broad student archetypes. Finer subgroup stories (for example, a fuel-vs-Engine quadrant) will need the larger T3 sample.
  • The one grouping that really separates students: ARC state. Of the groupings we tested, a student's ARC state is the variable that sorts the cohort most sharply on durable-skill scores. The effect is strongest on the Thrivers in Change pillar (resilience plus collaboration). The effect size here is substantial by conventional thresholds (see Statistical methods slide for epsilon-squared bands; the formal-analysis slides for the specific H and p values). Students flagged Growing or Start Here on ARC score meaningfully lower on that pillar than students flagged Strength. The formal-analysis slides show the full statistics. Practical read: how a student is doing on the ARC fuel side predicts their durable-skill profile better than how they do on the Engine side at this sample size.
  • What to tell stakeholders. The instrument is doing its job. It distinguishes students meaningfully across 27 cases, it surfaces the pattern the battery was designed to detect (strong skills on top, soft Engine and Fuel underneath), and it fires the clinical flag only on a genuine clinical picture. Coach-reported outcomes have not been collected yet; those will come with the T3 readout and will let us check whether the baseline drivers named above (the most common blocking ARC need and the most commonly constrained Engine subdomains) hold up in practice.
  • Caveats worth naming up front. n=27 is small. Cluster solutions, subgroup effect sizes, and two-peak (bimodality) tests in this deck should be read as suggestive, not definitive; a larger T3 sample will tighten every estimate here. Per-skill classifications use the unified Start Here / Growing / Strength bands (cutoffs at T=40 and T=55) that match the live C2A product as of 2026-04-21.

4. Glossary — terms used in this report

T-score
A standardized score where 50 is the published population average and every 10 points equals one standard deviation. T=60 means "about one SD above the reference mean," T=40 means "about one SD below." T-scores are used across all charts so every measure is on the same ruler.
Standard deviation (SD)
A measure of spread. SD bands on the radar show how tightly this cohort clusters around its mean. The published T-score scale is built so the reference population has SD=10, so cohort SDs near 10 mean our students are about as varied as the norming sample; larger SDs mean more spread, smaller means tighter.
Display bands (Start Here / Growing / Strength)
Three categorical bins used on skill bar charts: Start Here T<40, Growing 40≤T<55, Strength T≥55. As of 2026-04-21 these match the DDx thresholds used by the live C2A product (packages/assessment-engine/src/config/thresholds.ts) — one unified scale. Previously this report used a separate 30/60 display scale; that collided with the product's labels and has been retired.
Engine (C2A executive-function layer)
The three canonical executive-function subdomains that feed the DDx tiered matrix: working memory and inhibition (from the ADEXI instrument) plus cognitive flexibility (C2A-constructed measure). Rated on the T-score scale as ability scores. "Constrained" means T<55 on that subdomain — the student scores below the norm on that cognitive control process. Higher T = stronger Engine ability. Note: "executive function" remains the academic term; "Engine" is the C2A-specific layer name that matches the product's tier system.
Engine tier (0 / 1 / 2)
Count of Engine subdomains where the student is constrained (T<55), clamped at 2. Tier 0 = no subdomains constrained. Tier 1 = exactly one constrained. Tier 2 = two or more constrained. Used as one axis of the 3×3 DDx grid.
ARC (Autonomy, Relatedness, Competence)
The three psychological needs from Self-Determination Theory. The C2A battery measures each need on two facets: satisfaction (positive) and frustration (negative). T≥55 on satisfaction = the need is being met strongly; T≥55 on frustration = the need is actively being thwarted. Both facets feed the ARC state classification (start_here / growing / strength).
ARC state (start_here / growing / strength)
Rollup of the three ARC needs into a single developmental position. Strength: high satisfaction, low frustration across all three. Growing: mixed. Start here: frustration exceeds satisfaction on one or more needs (the "crisis" quadrant: satisfaction T<55 AND frustration T≥55). A need in the "conflicted" quadrant (both satisfaction and frustration high) rolls up to growing, not start_here — consistent with the frustration-exceeds-satisfaction rule since frustration does not exceed satisfaction in the conflicted quadrant. Used as the other axis of the 3×3 DDx grid.
3×3 DDx grid
Classification matrix: Engine tier (rows) × ARC state (columns) = 9 cells. Each cell maps to a coaching strategy. This is the LA3 headline chart.
GMS cascade
A clinical circuit breaker. It fires when a student scores low on both motivation scales simultaneously (intrinsic motivation T<40 AND amotivation T<40) AND has an ARC state that is not “strength” (i.e., at least one psychological need is frustrated or unmet). The clinical picture: the student is neither intrinsically engaged nor consistently disengaged; motivation is absent as a dominant orientation, while frustration is elevated. When this pattern is detected, the system overrides the normal 3×3 grid routing and flags the student for clinical_stabilization. Named for the Global Motivation Scale (GMS) instrument that drives the trigger.
Kruskal-Wallis (KW) test
A non-parametric test that asks "do these groups differ on this outcome?" without assuming the outcome is normally distributed. Used here because n=27 is too small to trust parametric ANOVA. H is the test statistic. p is the usual significance value. ε² (epsilon-squared) is the effect size (0 to 1; higher = more variance explained by the grouping).
Pearson vs Spearman correlation
Pearson measures linear association. Spearman measures rank association (monotonic but not necessarily linear). Reporting both is standard per de Winter (2016). Cells where they diverge by >0.10 flag potential non-linear relationships.
Silhouette score
A cluster-quality metric from -1 to 1. Higher = students are clearly closer to their assigned cluster than to any other cluster. Values above 0.40 are typically considered "reasonable" structure; below 0.25 is weak.
Hartigan dip test
A statistical test for bimodality (two peaks instead of one). Low p-value (<0.05) suggests the distribution has two modes. High p-value means "no evidence of two peaks."
PCA biplot
Principal Component Analysis reduces the 12-skill space to two axes that capture the most variance. PC1 is the axis of greatest spread, PC2 is the next. Arrows show which skills load on which axis. Useful for spotting gross structure; a biplot that separates cleanly by color suggests the color variable is related to the skill profile.
Ward linkage dendrogram
A tree showing how students cluster together by similarity. Branches fuse from the bottom up: students joined near the bottom are very similar, branches that fuse high up are very different. Cutting the tree at a chosen height gives you K clusters.

5. Cohort mean profile (radar, mean ± 1 SD)

In plain language: This radar shows the average student across all 12 skills, with a shaded band for ±1 standard deviation (about 68% of students fall inside the band on each skill). Each spoke is one skill; farther from the center = higher T-score.

The cohort clusters around T=60 across all 12 skills, closer to the top of the Growing band than the published T=50 center. The ±1 SD band is widest in the Thrivers-in-Change pillar (resilience, collaboration).

6. Per-pillar distributions (violin + box + strip)

In plain language: Each pillar has its own "shape" showing where students fall. The violin body shows the density of scores (wider = more students at that T level). The box inside is the interquartile range; the dot strip marks individual students.

p3 (Thrivers in Change) has the widest spread. p4 (Connectors & Collaborators) shows the tightest distribution and the lowest median. n=27 per pillar; jitter shows every student.

7. Skills by display band (Strength / Growing / Start Here)

In plain language: Each bar is one of the 12 skills, broken into three slices: how many students score in Strength (T≥55), Growing (40≤T<55), and Start Here (T<40). Skills are sorted so the ones with the most Strength students appear on the left. Display bands match the live C2A product thresholds as of 2026-04-21.

Ordered by descending Strength (T≥55) count. Every skill has a majority of students in the Strength band (16 to 22 of 27 per skill). The middle Growing band (40≤T<55) holds 4–9 students per skill, the coachable-opportunity group with real headroom to move into Strength. Four skills have zero students in Start Here (T<40): collaboration, critical thinking, curiosity, social awareness. Networking, creative problem solving, and communication carry the largest Start Here counts (2–3 students each).

8. LA3 headline: 3×3 DDx classification grid

In plain language: This is the headline clinical-routing chart. Rows are Engine tier (0 = no constrained Engine subdomains, 2 = two or more). Columns are ARC state (start_here / growing / strength). Each cell shows how many students land in that combination. The cell dictates which coaching strategy the DDx engine recommends.

2 students reach the ARC-state Strength column. Engine tier distribution is 1 / 0 / 26 (tier 0/1/2); 26/27 students are constrained on at least one Engine subdomain. ARC state: 14/27 start_here, 11/27 growing, 2/27 strength. Cascade count = 0 at this timepoint. See the annotated grid (next slide group) for per-cell blocking-need and constrained-subdomain decomposition.

9. Subdomain distributions: Engine + ARC need-level

In plain language: Nine panels on one T-score ruler. Row 1 shows the three Engine subdomains (working memory, inhibition, cognitive flexibility). Row 2 shows ARC satisfaction per need (autonomy, relatedness, competence). Row 3 shows ARC frustration per need (unreflected — higher = more frustrated). Reference lines mark T=40 (Start Here upper bound) and T=55 (Strength lower bound).

Engine subdomains all cluster below the T=50 norm, densest in the 35–50 band (working memory M=43.7, inhibition M=42.8, cognitive flexibility M=42.1). ARC satisfaction medians hover near 50 with considerable spread; ARC frustration means run autonomy 50.6, relatedness 52.7, competence 53.4 — relatedness frustration has the widest upper tail.

10. ARC driver: blocking-need frequency (non-Strength students)

In plain language: Which psychological need most often puts a student into the "crisis" quadrant (satisfaction T<55 AND frustration T≥55)? One bar per need, count of students in crisis among non-Strength students (n=25).

Relatedness is the most common blocking need — 9/25 non-Strength students are in crisis on this need (36%). Counts can exceed the denominator because a student can be in crisis on multiple needs simultaneously. This is the layer-level driver analysis: coaching teams should weight relational/belonging supports more heavily than autonomy-support on average for this cohort.

11. Engine driver: constrained-subdomain frequency (tier 1+2)

In plain language: Which Engine subdomain is most commonly constrained (T<55)? One bar per subdomain, count of students with T<55 among tier 1+2 students (n=26). Unclamped: a student with all three constrained contributes to all three bars.

Inhibition and cognitive flexibility are tied as the most commonly constrained Engine subdomain — 25/26 tier 1+2 students (96%). Near-universal constraints across the Engine layer suggest coaching work should foreground executive-function scaffolding broadly, not just one subdomain.

12. DDx grid with per-cell driver decomposition

In plain language: The same 3×3 grid, with each non-empty cell annotated for the dominant blocking ARC need (short-form: Aut/Rel/Com) + dominant constrained Engine subdomain (WM/Inh/CF). Tiebreaks list all tied items. Tier-0 cells omit the constrained row (no Engine constraints to report); Strength cells omit the blocking row (no ARC need is in crisis by definition). Cell fill encodes student count per cell.

Reads as a coaching-plan cheat sheet: each non-empty cell suggests where to start. The largest cell is Tier 2 x Start Here (n=14) — see the annotated chart for the dominant blocking need + constrained subdomain in each cell.

13. Motivational state breakdown (5 categories)

In plain language: Motivation isn't a single dial — it combines intrinsic motivation (wanting to engage) and amotivation (disengagement). This stacked bar shows the 5 blended states. "Strong intrinsic" = highly engaged, low disengaged. "Cascade" = the clinical circuit breaker (see Glossary for the full three-condition trigger).

16/27 students sit in mixed motivation (both amotivation and intrinsic motivation in the Growing band). Five students register strong_intrinsic; zero hit the GMS cascade trigger at T1.

14. Per-skill histograms

In plain language: One histogram per skill. The x-axis is T-score, the y-axis is how many students fall in each bucket. A clean bell shape means the scores are well-spread; a tall narrow peak means students cluster around a single level.

All 12 distributions cluster around T=60 ± 10. No visible bimodality. Shapiro-Wilk p-values (see stderr log) are mixed at n=27 — about half the skills are non-normal at p<0.05, the other half pass the test — consistent with a small sample where the test has limited power and where modest departures from normality do not invalidate the descriptive picture.

15. Per-pillar violins (all 4 pillars)

In plain language: One violin per pillar, showing the full distribution of per-student pillar means. A tall narrow violin = students agree; a fat violin = students disagree. The white dot is the median.

Pillar means: p1=61.3, p2=58.3, p3=63.6 (widest spread), p4=57.1. Three of the four pillars (p1, p2, p3) include students both above T=70 and below T=45; p4 is narrower, ranging roughly T=38 to T=68.

16. Per-skill violins, grouped by pillar

In plain language: One violin per skill, grouped and color-coded by pillar. Lets you see which specific skills drive each pillar's shape.

Adaptability and curiosity (both p1) run highest. Networking (p4) runs lowest. Pillar color-coding: navy=p1, coral=p2, plum=p3, forest=p4.

17. Hierarchically clustered student × skill heatmap

In plain language: A grid where each row is a student and each column is a skill. Color intensity encodes T-score (dark = high, light = low). Students and skills are reordered so similar rows and columns sit next to each other, revealing visual "blocks" where groups of students excel together.

Reveals two broad blocks: a high-scoring cluster (warm cells dominating the rows) and a lower-scoring cluster with scattered low cells. Consistent with K=2 silhouette dominance in the profile-patterns analysis.

18. PCA biplot (12-skill space, 27 students, colored by ARC state)

In plain language: Principal Component Analysis squeezes the 12-skill space into 2 axes that capture the most variance. Each dot is one student. Arrows show which skills pull students in which direction on the two axes. Color = ARC state.

PC1 separates cohort along a global-proficiency axis (all skill loadings point the same direction). PC2 separates cognitive skills from interpersonal. start_here students (coral) do not cleanly separate from growing (gold) on PC axes.

19. Small-multiples radar grid (5×6, all 27 students)

In plain language: 27 mini radar charts, one per student, on a shared scale. Lets you scan for shape diversity across the cohort at a glance. Radar color indicates each student's ARC state.

Each polar chart is one student's 12-skill profile. Strong heterogeneity visible: some students are uniformly high, others have jagged profiles with one or two pronounced dips. Radar color reflects ARC state.

20. Hierarchical dendrogram (Ward linkage)

In plain language: A tree showing how students cluster by similarity. Branches fuse from the bottom up: students joined near the bottom are very similar, branches that fuse high up are very different. Cutting the tree at a horizontal line yields K clusters.

Dashed horizontal line = K=3 cut height. Three clusters of sizes roughly 12 / 9 / 6 emerge but with moderate agglomerative distance — n=27 is small for stable dendrogram interpretation.

21. K-means clustering: K=2 vs K=3 side-by-side

In plain language: Two side-by-side views of the same PCA scatter, colored once by a 2-group assignment and once by a 3-group assignment. Silhouette scores (shown in each title) quantify how cleanly the groups separate; higher is better.

K=2 silhouette (0.40) > K=3 silhouette (0.26) — the two-cluster structure is tighter. K=2 splits into a larger core (20) and an outlier group (7); K=3 further splits the core into groups of 12 / 9 / 6.

22. K=3 cluster mean profiles with ±1 SD bands

In plain language: Each line is one cluster's mean profile across the 12 skills, with a shaded ±1 SD band. If the lines had very different shapes (one cluster high on cognitive, low on interpersonal; another the opposite), that would suggest genuinely different archetypes. If they're mostly parallel, the clusters differ in overall level but not in shape.

Cluster 0 (lowest mean) averages ~T=50 across skills; cluster 1 mid-range; cluster 2 (highest) clusters above T=65. Shape differences across clusters are smaller than height differences — clusters are primarily separated by level, not profile shape.

23. Silhouette score by K (K=2..6)

In plain language: How well-separated are the clusters at each choice of K (number of groups)? Higher silhouette = cleaner separation. The peak tells you the best K for this cohort.

K=2 wins decisively. This is a caveat on the "fuel vs engine" hypothesis: at n=27 we cannot cleanly distinguish 3+ profile types.

24. Correlation matrix: Pearson vs Spearman (12×12)

In plain language: Two 12×12 correlation matrices. The left shows Pearson (linear) correlations; the right shows Spearman (rank-based). Cell color encodes correlation strength; red = positive, blue = negative. Reporting both is standard practice — they agree when the relationship is linear and diverge when it's curved or distorted by outliers.

Both matrices show strong positive inter-skill correlations (halo effect common in self-report). Pearson and Spearman agree on sign throughout.

25. Pearson-Spearman divergence (|Δ| > 0.10 flagged)

In plain language: The difference between Pearson and Spearman correlations, cell-by-cell. Cells marked with * have |Δ| > 0.10, which de Winter (2016) flags as "potentially non-linear" — worth a closer look before assuming the relationship is a straight line.

Asterisk cells mark where rank correlation diverges from linear by >0.10 (de Winter 2016). Few cells flag, suggesting linear relationships fit the skill-level T-scores reasonably well.

26. Kruskal-Wallis by Engine tier (cascade excluded)

In plain language: For each skill and pillar, a Kruskal-Wallis test asks "do the three Engine tier groups have different scores?" Green bars = stronger effect, gray = weaker. Reads H, p, ε² off the chart. Cascade-positive students are excluded from this test; tier counts are 1 / 0 / 26 for tier 0/1/2.

Most outcomes do not reach p<0.05. Engine tier group sizes are imbalanced (1 / 0 / 26) which limits power — with 1 tier-0 and 0 tier-1 students in this cohort, only one group is well-populated. Interpret group-difference effects cautiously; the distribution itself is the more informative finding.

27. Kruskal-Wallis by ARC state

In plain language: Same test as the Engine tier chart, but grouped by ARC state (start_here / growing / strength) instead. This is the test that picks up the strongest signals at n=27.

Strongest pillar-level effects (ARC-state groups): pillar_p2 H=12.55 p=0.002 ε²=0.44; pillar_p1 H=11.57 p=0.003 ε²=0.40; pillar_p3 H=10.18 p=0.006 ε²=0.34; pillar_p4 H=6.62 p=0.037 ε²=0.19. All four pillars separate by ARC state at n=27. ARC fuel state is more discriminating than Engine tier at this sample size because tier distribution is highly imbalanced.

28. Hartigan dip test (pillar means + PC1)

In plain language: Hartigan's dip test asks "is this distribution bimodal (two peaks) or unimodal (one peak)?" A low p-value is evidence for two peaks. High p = the distribution is consistent with having just one mode. Run on each pillar mean and on the first principal component of the skill space.

All dip p-values > 0.5 — no evidence of bimodality on any pillar mean or on PC1 of the 12-skill space. The "two-mode" hypothesis is not supported at n=27.

29. Exemplar S01: high-variance profile

In plain language: One student's four-panel profile card: radar of all 12 skills, Engine subdomain bar chart, ARC satisfaction-vs-frustration scatter, and GMS motivation state badge. S01 is the student whose per-skill SD was the highest in the cohort — in other words, the most uneven profile.

Max per-skill SD across 12 skills. Jagged radar indicates uneven development across durable skills. Engine and ARC panels contextualize why some skills may be constrained.

30. Exemplar S11: near-median profile

In plain language: The student whose overall mean T-score is closest to the cohort median — the "typical" case, useful for anchoring stakeholder narration. "This is what a normal student in this cohort looks like."

Mean-T exactly matches cohort median (61.24). Demonstrates the "typical" shape for narration — radar close to circular at Growing band, balanced ARC quadrants.

31. Exemplar S14: ARC-stressed (highest relatedness frustration, T=85)

In plain language: The student with the highest single-need ARC frustration score in the cohort. If anyone had triggered the GMS cascade we would show them here instead, but cascade count = 0 at T1, so we show the next closest proxy — a student a coach would want to prioritize.

Cascade count = 0 at T1, so this panel substitutes the highest ARC-frustration student. Relatedness frustration at T=85, with the relatedness need both frustrated and unmet simultaneously; a coaching-priority candidate even absent a full cascade.

32. Caveats & statistical context

  • Completer-only sample. 27 of 46 NLU students assigned to T1 completed the full battery. The 19 non-completers are not in any analysis on this deck. Inferences about the full NLU cohort (and about future cohorts) depend on completers being representative of non-completers. At n=27 this is not statistically testable; a meaningful check awaits a larger sample at T3.
  • n=27 is small. Every number in this deck should be read as exploratory. Effect sizes lead the narrative; p-values are directional evidence, not claims.
  • No multiple-testing correction applied. With 16 outcomes tested across 2 grouping factors at α=0.05, ~1.6 false-positive "significant" results are expected by chance. Findings should replicate at T3 before being treated as confirmed.
  • K=2 beats K=3 in clustering. The "fuel vs engine" coaching-archetype hypothesis is not supported as a three-group solution at this n. A bigger cohort at T3 may reveal more structure.
  • Zero cascade-positive students at T1. No student routes to clinical_stabilization this round. Exemplar 3 substitutes the highest single-need ARC frustration.
  • 2 students reach ARC Strength in the 3×3 grid — a small minority. 25 of 27 have at least one ARC need in Start Here or Growing. Interpretable as "most students have some fuel constraint at baseline" — clinically meaningful and coaching-actionable.
  • NaN-leak fix applied in `_common.py:reclassify_students`. Prior LA3 runs silently let NaN demo-instrument rows clobber real values via pandas last-write-wins; NaN then leaked through is None guards into Engine tier (where NaN<55 returns False, producing a silent "unconstrained" default). The 2026-04-21 fix adds dropna before the lookup and pd.isna-aware value retrieval, producing the tier distribution 1 / 0 / 26 from the raw subscales CSV. Engine tier numbers from earlier bugged runs should be considered superseded.
  • ARC inversion correction applied. BMPN dissatisfaction subscales are reflected in the scoring pipeline (high T = LOW frustration). A latent bug at apps/server/services/c2a/profile-generation-service.ts:637-639 passes reflected T directly to ARC quadrant logic, which inverts frustration. This analysis uses the corrected derivation (T_unreflected = 100 − T_reflected) for ARC state and exemplars. Production counterparts of these figures will differ until the bug is fixed.
  • No trend data yet. T3 data will give us change scores and will sharpen every effect estimate here.
